Active Work Architecture
─[ Ecosystem Readiness and Hardening ]─ ┐

Ecosystem Readiness and Hardening

Goal

Prepare the multi-tier system for enterprise evaluation and team-scale adoption. This plan owns ecosystem hardening that is specific to multi-tier operation — fleet governance, cross-tier trust, and scale readiness concerns that don’t apply to standalone deployments.

Context

An investor/architect review rated Lore highly on architecture clarity, safety controls, and test discipline, while identifying ecosystem maturity gaps that become critical at multi-tier scale.

Relationship to standalone plans: General OSS readiness (governance, security baseline, release discipline, first-run experience) is owned by the Standalone Go-Live Positioning roadmap. This plan builds on top of that foundation with multi-tier-specific concerns.

Scope

1. Multi-tier adoption framing and messaging

  • Define clear, honest messaging for each deployment tier:
    • Standalone: stable, recommended starting point
    • 2-tier: early access, pilot-validated (after pilot completes)
    • 3-tier: experimental, use at own risk
  • Ensure all docs, README, and launch materials use consistent tier language
  • Document the standalone → 2-tier migration path for teams that start standalone and grow

2. Enterprise evaluation readiness

  • Prepare for enterprise security review questions:
    • Multi-tier trust model: what can a child repo do to a parent?
    • Contribution review: what does a maintainer need to check before merging?
    • Data flow: what crosses tier boundaries and what stays local?
  • Document isolation boundaries between tiers
  • Prepare answers for: “Can a compromised instance affect the team repo?“

3. Fleet governance at scale

  • Define recommended governance model for 5+ instances:
    • Contribution review cadence expectations
    • Conflict resolution escalation path
    • Stale instance detection and cleanup
  • Document when PR-first vs direct-mode is appropriate at each scale tier
  • Define maintainer burden expectations (contributions/week, review time)

4. Cross-tier observability (evaluation only)

  • Evaluate lightweight fleet health signals:
    • Which instances last synced, and when
    • Which instances have unresolved conflicts
    • Contribution backlog (PRs pending review)
  • Determine if this is worth building or if validate-consistency.sh per-repo is sufficient
  • Decision: build only if pilot reveals observability gaps

5. Multi-tier onboarding documentation

  • “Day 1 as a team member” guide: receive instance → sync → first session → contribute back
  • Troubleshooting guide for common multi-tier issues (beyond sync-troubleshooting.md)
  • Maintainer onboarding: “You’ve been made team maintainer — here’s what that means”

Dependencies

  • Requires standalone go-live baseline to be complete (governance, security, release)
  • Requires pilot data for evidence-backed tier messaging
  • Fleet governance scope informed by pilot conflict/contribution frequency data

Success Criteria

  • Tier messaging (standalone/2-tier/3-tier) is consistent across all docs and launch materials
  • Enterprise evaluation Q&A is documented (trust model, data flow, isolation)
  • Fleet governance model is defined for 5+ instance scale
  • Standalone → 2-tier migration path is documented
  • Multi-tier onboarding guides exist (team member, maintainer)
  • Cross-tier observability evaluation is complete with build/defer decision

Risks

RiskMitigation
Enterprise hardening without enterprise users is speculativeKeep scope to documentation and messaging; defer tooling until demand
Fleet governance creates overhead that discourages small-team adoptionFrame as “recommended for 5+ instances” — small teams can skip
Cross-tier observability becomes a build-before-need trapEvaluate only; build only if pilot data justifies

Test Plan

bash scripts/validate-consistency.sh
mkdocs build --strict

Manual verification:

  • Confirm tier language is consistent across README, docs homepage, quickstart, and roadmap summaries
  • Confirm enterprise Q&A addresses real security reviewer concerns
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
